dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for an overdose at a residence in [CITY].
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT. Who called 911 and what happened before we got here?
The patient was unconscious before we arrived and was unresponsive for about five minutes. She is now alert and said she may have ingested something.
She was initially unresponsive (GCS 3) but is now alert, oriented to person, place, and time.
Pulse is currently 80 with a respiratory rate of 11. We're supporting her breathing with a BVM and 15 LPM oxygen.
Airway is clear and patent. Breathing was initially depressed, but it is becoming more spontaneous now.
We used a BVM with 15 LPM of oxygen per protocol to support her low respiratory rate.
We placed a 28-gauge NPA in the right nostril to keep the airway open and aid breathing.
Chest is symmetric and non-traumatic. No signs of tracheal deviation or jugular vein distention.
Skin is pink, warm, and clammy. Normal turgor, no rashes or swelling.
Pupils were initially pinpoint but are now equal, round, and reactive to light - PEARL.
We started an 18-gauge IV in her left arm to allow for rapid administration of medication as needed.
A 4-lead ECG was placed to monitor heart rhythm continuously.
We administered 0.5 mg of Narcan IV, which improved her respiratory rate and level of consciousness significantly.
Initial BP was 103/67. It has increased slightly to 139/78 in the latest reading.
Head is normocephalic with no deformities. No signs of trauma or injury.
Most recent vitals include BP 139/78, pulse 89, RR 30, oxygen saturation 94%, and EKG shows sinus rhythm.
She takes Clonidine, Hydrochlorothiazide, Amlodipine, Lisinopril, and Ibuprofen. She also mentioned Xanax. Any other medications or supplements?
She is also taking Vitamin A and Vitamin D supplements.
She denies current drug use. Her partner mentioned a history of drug use over 10 years ago. Any concerns about relapse?
Pelvis is intact with no signs of instability, tenderness, or trauma.
Glucose was high at 315 mg/dL initially and later became undetectable.
Circulation is normal. No bleeding or poor peripheral pulses observed.
Extremities show no abnormalities. Sensation and pulses are intact and normal.
Back and flank are unremarkable. No signs of injury or deformity.
Head and neck look normal. Pupils are reactive and not fixed or dilated.
No obstruction, drainage, or injuries noted in the ears, nose, or mouth.
Chest movement is symmetrical and atraumatic. Breath sounds are normal bilaterally.
Abdomen is soft and unremarkable with normal bowel sounds. No rectal bleeding or urinary issues.
Oxygen saturation was 100% initially but dropped to 94% after assisted breaths are initiated.
She is now fully alert, oriented, and has a GCS of 15 with no neurological deficits.
First EKG not available, but the most recent showed a normal sinus rhythm.
She reported no pain on a 0-10 scale. Let us know if anything changes.
Repeating the S.A.M.P.L.E. didn't reveal anything new. Her condition appears consistent.
She declined transport and signed an AMA form. If symptoms return or worsen, call 911.
Since she declined transport, we are staying on scene. A backup plan is if she deteriorates.
Vitals remain stable: BP 139/78, pulse 89, and SpO2 94%.
No pain reported. No new symptoms. She remains stable.
IV line and ECG have been removed. She is now undressed from all medical devices.
Case is being documented and officially closed. All equipment is cleaned and secured.
She was informed about the importance of calling 911 if symptoms recur or worsen.
All equipment is off and the site is reassessed for any complications.
She chose to stay at home with written AMA documentation and a signed refusal form.
